task_prompt: An NLP team built a sentiment classifier for product reviews and compared it against a legacy rule-based system. They reported 88% accuracy on the test set versus 71% for the baseline. However, the team used TF-IDF features computed on the full dataset (train + test combined) before splitting. The baseline system only processes the test split. The new model's features therefore include information derived from test-set vocabulary frequencies, while the baseline operates on raw text. Both systems were evaluated on the same 10,000-review held-out test set. The team concludes that the neural classifier outperforms the rule-based system by 17 percentage points. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The TF-IDF vectorizer was fit on the full dataset including test examples, constituting data leakage that inflates the neural model's reported accuracy.

planted_issues.description: TF-IDF was fit on train+test combined, leaking test-set vocabulary statistics into the model's feature space and inflating accuracy.

ideal_critique: The TF-IDF vectorizer should be fit only on training data; fitting on train+test leaks document-frequency statistics from the test set into the feature representation.
The 17pp improvement cannot be attributed to the model because the evaluation is confounded by the preprocessing leak.
ideal_defense: The defense should concede the preprocessing leak fully and propose refitting the vectorizer on training data only before rerunning the comparison.
The defense may note the model architecture could still outperform, but cannot claim the 17pp figure is valid.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Refitting TF-IDF on training data only and re-evaluating produces a smaller or non-significant gap.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Refitting TF-IDF on training data only produces the same 17pp gap, indicating the leak did not inflate results.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The gap shrinks but remains statistically significant.

ground_truth_rationale: Fitting TF-IDF on train+test is a textbook preprocessing leak. The reported accuracy cannot be trusted as a valid comparison. The critique is unambiguously correct and the case has a single clear flaw.
notes: Classic preprocessing leakage case. Directly stated in the scenario. Easy baseline.

task_prompt: A fraud detection team introduced a gradient boosting classifier to replace a threshold-based rule system. To measure improvement, they selected the classification threshold for the new model by maximizing F1 on the held-out test set, then reported the test-set F1. The legacy rule system uses a fixed, pre-specified threshold. The new model achieved F1 = 0.83 versus the rule system's F1 = 0.71. The team reports a statistically significant improvement and recommends full deployment. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The threshold for the new model was selected by maximizing F1 on the test set, which optimistically biases the reported F1 and makes the comparison with the rule system invalid.

planted_issues.description: The new model's threshold was chosen to maximize F1 on the test set, which constitutes test-set threshold optimization and overstates real-world performance.

ideal_critique: Threshold selection must be done on a validation set, not the test set; optimizing threshold on the test set inflates the reported F1.
The comparison with the rule-system's fixed threshold is unfair because the model's threshold was post-hoc optimized.
ideal_defense: Defense should concede threshold selection on test set is invalid and propose using a held-out validation set for threshold selection before retesting.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Re-selecting threshold on a validation set and re-evaluating on a separate test set produces a smaller gap between the model and rule system.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Re-selecting threshold on a validation set still yields F1 = 0.83 or higher, preserving the claimed improvement.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Gap shrinks to within confidence intervals.

ground_truth_rationale: Optimizing the classification threshold on the test set is explicitly stated and is a clear violation of proper evaluation protocol. This is a directly observable single-signal flaw.
notes: Threshold-on-test leakage, explicitly stated. Easy detection case.

task_prompt: A churn prediction team compared their new gradient boosting model against a logistic regression baseline. The new model achieved AUC = 0.89 with 95% CI [0.87, 0.91] and the baseline achieved AUC = 0.82 with CI [0.79, 0.85]. The team notes the CIs do not overlap and concludes the difference is statistically significant. Both models were trained on 18 months of data and evaluated on a subsequent 3-month holdout. However, the gradient boosting model underwent extensive hyperparameter tuning using a random search with 200 trials, with the best configuration selected based on validation AUC. The logistic regression used default sklearn parameters. The team's recommendation is to deploy the gradient boosting model. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The comparison is confounded by hyperparameter optimization: the gradient boosting model was extensively tuned on validation data while the baseline used default parameters, making the measured gap attributable to tuning rather than model class differences.

planted_issues.description: The gradient boosting model received 200-trial hyperparameter search while the logistic regression used defaults, making the performance gap attributable to tuning effort rather than model quality.
Non-overlapping 95% CIs for AUC does not directly imply significance of the difference between AUC values; the appropriate test is on the difference, not on individual CIs.

The memo frames the result as gradient boosting superiority, but the study only compares heavily tuned GBM against untuned default logistic regression, so the causal interpretation of the gap is overstated.

ideal_critique: The 200-trial hyperparameter search for GBM versus default parameters for LR is an unfair comparison; LR should also be tuned.
Non-overlapping individual CIs does not establish significance of the AUC difference; a paired test on the difference is required.
ideal_defense: Default LR parameters are a legitimate baseline for operational comparison if the team's intent is to measure the value of using GBM as-deployed versus LR as-deployed.
If the deployment scenario is GBM tuned vs LR default, this is a fair comparison for the business decision, though not for a model-class comparison.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Tuning LR closes the AUC gap to within the CI of the difference.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Tuned LR still underperforms GBM by the same margin.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Tuned LR closes part of the gap but not all.

ground_truth_rationale: The scenario explicitly states the tuning asymmetry and the CI interpretation, but connecting them both as issues requires recognizing the CI overlap fallacy which is an additional reasoning step beyond just finding the tuning asymmetry.
notes: Two issues: tuning asymmetry (stated) and CI overlap fallacy (implicit). Medium because both require synthesis.

task_prompt: A recommendation systems team compared a new collaborative filtering model against a popularity baseline. They evaluated both systems using offline A/B testing on historical data: for each user session in the test set, they replayed the session and measured whether the target item appeared in the top-10 recommendations. The new model achieved Hit Rate@10 = 0.43 versus 0.31 for the popularity baseline. CIs were computed using bootstrap over sessions (N=200,000) and do not overlap. However, the team trained both models on data from January–September, and the test set consists of October–December sessions. The popularity baseline's rankings are computed from January–September purchase frequencies. The new model uses these same frequencies as one of its features. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The popularity baseline's rankings come from the same period as the training data, but if the test period (October–December) has different popularity patterns, both models are disadvantaged equally; however, the replay evaluation does not account for position bias and exposure bias from the original ranker, making the offline metric an unreliable predictor of online lift.

planted_issues.description: Replay-based offline evaluation measures HR@10 only for items the user interacted with, which were exposed by the original ranker; items the model would have recommended but weren't shown can never be positive, systematically penalizing models that differ from the original ranker.
Hit Rate@10 in offline replay has low correlation with online CTR/engagement due to exposure and position bias, undermining the deployment decision.

ideal_critique: Replay evaluation conflates impressions with recommendations; items not shown by the original system can never be correct answers, biasing against novel recommendations.
Offline hit rate has well-documented poor correlation with online A/B test outcomes in recommendation systems.
ideal_defense: The large session count (N=200k) makes sampling variance negligible; the evaluation is at least consistent across the two models.
Popularity baseline is a standard offline comparison point even if it doesn't fully capture online dynamics.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: An online A/B test shows the new model's CTR lift is not proportional to the offline HR@10 gap, or shows no significant lift.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: An online A/B test confirms statistically significant CTR lift consistent with the offline gap.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Online lift is positive but smaller than offline gap suggests.

ground_truth_rationale: Replay evaluation bias is well-known in recommender systems research but requires domain knowledge to articulate. The offline-online gap is an additional issue. An online A/B test would be the definitive resolution.
notes: Recommender systems domain knowledge needed. Medium because replay evaluation is somewhat known but requires articulation.

task_prompt:
PRODUCT INTELLIGENCE QUARTERLY REVIEW — Q3 CONFIDENTIAL

The Platform Integrity team is pleased to report the successful completion of the AURA project, our next-generation content ranking upgrade, which has now entered a staged rollout following a rigorous 8-week evaluation program.

The evaluation team, led by Dr. Priya Nair (Staff Research Scientist), designed a prospective comparison of the AURA ranker against our existing production system across a stratified sample of content categories. The evaluation was powered to detect a 2.5% relative improvement in 7-day content engagement rate at 80% power and a two-sided α of 0.05. Power calculations were performed using a one-sample z-test approximation with the estimated engagement rate variance from our prior 90-day baseline period (σ² = 0.0041).

Across the 6-week primary evaluation window, the AURA ranker achieved a 7-day engagement rate of 34.7% versus 33.4% for the production baseline — a 1.3 percentage point lift corresponding to a 3.9% relative improvement. The per-session sample size was N = 1,840,000 across both arms, split approximately equally. The team reports z = 4.31, p < 0.0001, 95% CI [+0.9pp, +1.7pp]. The result exceeds the pre-specified 2.5% relative target, confirming AURA's superiority under the evaluation design.

Before running the evaluation, the team validated that the variance estimate from the baseline period was appropriate for use in the power calculation: the baseline period's σ² = 0.0041 derives from session-level engagement data aggregated at the day-session grain, where each observation corresponds to a unique daily session. The team noted that individual users appear on average 6.2 times across the 90-day baseline and argued that because sessions are the unit of analysis, independence at the session level is sufficient for the z-test.

Infrastructure notes: AURA uses a two-tower retrieval architecture (user tower / content tower) with a cross-attention reranker at the top-K stage. Serving latency improved by 12ms at p99 compared to the previous ensemble approach, which is a meaningful infrastructure win for the mobile experience.

The evaluation was reviewed by the Data Science Review Board on September 14th. The recommendation for staged rollout was approved unanimously. Phase 1 rollout (20% of eligible users) begins October 1st. Full rollout is scheduled for November 15th, pending no regression signals in monitoring dashboards. The appendix to the memo includes routine implementation details — staffing plans, rollout checkpoints, dashboard ownership, and escalation pathways — because the organization is preparing for deployment rather than for an academic publication. Those details are deliberately realistic but methodologically secondary. They create the texture of a credible internal review and force the reader to distinguish operational confidence from actual evidentiary support for the claim. Those details are deliberately realistic but methodologically secondary. They create the texture of a credible internal review and force the reader to distinguish operational confidence from actual evidentiary support for the claim. ground_truth.final_verdict: The z-test assumes independence of observations, but users appear 6.2 times on average across the evaluation window, creating intraclass correlation at the user level; the reported z-statistic and p-value are anti-conservative because the effective sample size is far smaller than 1,840,000 sessions.

planted_issues.description: Treating individual sessions as independent observations when users contribute on average 6.2 sessions apiece violates the iid assumption; the effective N is approximately N_users, not N_sessions, making the reported z=4.31 anti-conservative.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that intraclass correlation in repeated-measures data inflates type I error when naive z-tests are applied to session-level data; requires understanding of clustered standard errors or user-level analysis as the correct unit of randomization.

planted_issues.description: The power calculation used session-level variance (σ²=0.0041) but this variance underestimates between-user variation; the correct variance for the power calculation should use user-level averages, not session-level observations.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowledge that variance of a session-level mean is not the same as variance of a user-level mean when sessions are clustered within users; σ² at session level ≠ σ² at user level.

planted_issues.description: The reported confidence interval is computed as though 1.84M sessions were independent, so its width is mechanically too narrow relative to a user-clustered analysis.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing how clustered dependence narrows naive standard errors and confidence intervals.

ideal_critique: The unit of randomization is the session, but users appear 6.2x on average; this is a clustered data structure that requires clustered standard errors or user-level analysis — not a session-level z-test.
The variance estimate σ²=0.0041 was computed from session-level data and underestimates user-level variance due to within-user correlation; the power calculation and CI are both anti-conservative.
The effective sample size is approximately N_users, not 1.84M sessions; the true z-statistic is likely substantially lower and the result may not be significant at the pre-specified alpha.
ideal_defense: The team should concede that session-level z-tests are inappropriate when users appear multiple times; the correct analysis is a user-level average or a mixed-effects model.
The defense may note the practical result (1.3pp lift) may still be real, but the statistical inference is invalid as reported.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Reanalyzing at the user level (computing per-user engagement rate and performing a two-sample t-test on user averages) produces a substantially larger CI or non-significant result.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: User-level reanalysis confirms z > 3.5 and the CI remains [+0.9pp, +1.7pp], showing the session-level bias had negligible practical impact.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: User-level analysis is significant but the CI is wider (e.g., [+0.3pp, +2.3pp]), changing the precision but not the conclusion.

ground_truth_rationale: The iid assumption violation (users appearing 6.2x) creates clustered data that invalidates a session-level z-test. The variance estimate uses the wrong grain. Both issues require quantitative reasoning about effective sample sizes. The document explicitly justifies the session-level test, providing a subtly wrong justification that a careful reviewer must evaluate and reject.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 2. Flaw type A (assumption violation: iid) + B (quantitative error: variance grain). The document is internally consistent — every number is correct given the session-level analysis; the error is in the assumed relationship between sessions and independent units. The justification ('sessions are the unit of analysis') is subtly wrong: it conflates the analysis unit with the randomization unit. A reviewer must evaluate this justification (step 4 of Principle 4). IDR: both issues require understanding clustered inference. IDP: 'six-week window too short' is a red herring. DC: the correct verdict requires computing effective N, not just noting the 6.2x repetition. DRQ: critique_wins (no empirical test needed; the math is wrong). ETD: would need user-level reanalysis. FVC: the reported CI is anti-conservative, not just imprecise.
notes: Hard case: unit of analysis vs unit of randomization. Intraclass correlation from repeated sessions. Must not claim: evaluation duration, stratification issues. Red herring: 12ms latency improvement is distracting operational detail.

task_prompt: RELEASE READINESS MEMO — CLAIMS PIPELINE MODERNIZATION. The insurance claims ML group recommends replacing the current fraud screening model with the newly trained Atlas model before the January renewals peak. The evaluation compared Atlas against the production rule-plus-logistic system on a three-month held-out claims stream sampled from commercial auto and workers compensation lines. Atlas achieved AUCPR = 0.271 versus 0.214 for production and reduced manual review volume by 14% at the selected operating point. The report emphasizes that both systems were scored on the same claims and that thresholds were fixed before scoring. What changed operationally is described as a routine pipeline cleanup: duplicate provider records are now consolidated before feature generation, and procedure codes arriving under alternate payer aliases are standardized to a canonical mapping. These changes were applied in the Atlas scoring service. The production baseline continued to consume the legacy feature feed because engineering did not back-port the cleanup to the old stack during the evaluation window. The memo argues this is acceptable because the baseline represents the as-deployed system and the business decision is whether Atlas-as-built beats production-as-deployed. The quality section adds that duplicate consolidation mostly affects only 2.6% of claims and is therefore unlikely to explain the observed lift. The review board is told the systems agree on 91% of low-risk claims and that the disagreement mass is concentrated in specialist-billing patterns, which Atlas was designed to capture. The rollout request asks for immediate migration of all commercial lines with a two-week shadow period. Operational details were included in the review packet because the program is already resourced for launch: engineering capacity is allocated for the next release train, operations has staffed the monitoring rota, and finance has provisionally reserved budget contingent on a methods sign-off. The memo circulated among the data science review board, product leadership, and domain stakeholders. The writing tone is intentionally confident because the project has already passed infrastructure review and the remaining question is whether the evidence supports the claimed methodological conclusion. The document also notes that the team reviewed obvious concerns from prior launches: data source continuity, test environment stability, and reporting consistency. Those checks all passed and are presented as reassurance to stakeholders, which makes the remaining methodological flaw easy to miss on a quick read. Stakeholder comments in the appendix describe the result as strong enough for a phased rollout, but do not add new evidence. This operational confidence is part of the scenario noise: the methodology sounds polished and complete, and the critical issue is in an assumption or omission that is not obvious from surface-level review. ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported uplift cannot be attributed to the new model because the baseline and challenger differ in both model family and upstream deduplication logic, and the upstream change alone can account for the apparent gain.

planted_issues.description: The challenger is evaluated on a cleaned feature feed while the baseline remains on the legacy feed, so the comparison conflates model improvement with upstream data standardization.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that upstream entity-resolution and code-normalization changes can materially change downstream fraud features even when the model is unchanged.

planted_issues.description: Provider deduplication alters counts, recency, and provider-history features that are central to fraud detection; treating this as a trivial cleanup understates its methodological impact.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires fraud-analytics knowledge about provider-centric features and how alias consolidation affects them.

planted_issues.description: If the claim is Atlas model superiority, the study is invalid; if the claim is Atlas system superiority, the memo must attribute the gain to the combined system change rather than the model alone.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires distinguishing model comparison from end-to-end system comparison in production ML evaluations.

ideal_critique: The baseline and challenger do not share the same upstream feature generation pipeline, so the measured gain is not identifiable as a model effect.
Calling duplicate consolidation a routine cleanup is a subtly wrong justification because fraud features are highly sensitive to entity resolution and alias mapping.
A fair model comparison requires rerunning the legacy model on the same cleaned feed or Atlas on the legacy feed.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue the business decision concerns system replacement, not pure model science, but then the claim must be narrowed to end-to-end system performance.
The defense should concede that the memo over-attributes the gain to Atlas rather than the combined pipeline change.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Running the legacy model on the cleaned feed recovers most of the observed AUCPR lift, showing preprocessing rather than the model drives the result.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Running the legacy model on the cleaned feed leaves almost the full AUCPR gap intact, supporting a genuine model improvement.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Both the cleaned-feed legacy model and Atlas improve, but Atlas still retains a smaller residual gain.

ground_truth_rationale: This case is about broken baseline comparability created by an upstream entity-resolution change. The memo is internally consistent and even offers a plausible defense, but the claim direction is wrong unless the comparison is reframed as system-level rather than model-level.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 6, 8. Type D: the memo acknowledges the preprocessing difference and incorrectly minimizes it as trivial cleanup. Single-pass reviewers may flag the obvious red herring (three-month holdout) and miss the real comparability flaw. IDR, DC, and DRQ are all likely failure points.
notes: Hard BB case with interacting issues: upstream deduplication changes both feature distribution and attribution of the claimed gain. Domain red herrings are embedded in the text.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The evaluation mixes a model change with a retrieval-candidate-set change, so the current evidence cannot cleanly distinguish whether the new reranker is better or merely operating on a stronger candidate pool.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run an ablation matrix in which the old and new rerankers are each evaluated on both the legacy and new candidate generators, holding thresholds and business rules fixed.

planted_issues.description: The comparison changes both the retrieval candidate pool and the reranker, so the reported gain is not identifiable as a reranker effect.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires search-ranking knowledge that reranker quality is conditional on the candidate set it receives.

planted_issues.description: The largest improvements occur precisely where semantic retrieval would be expected to help most, suggesting the observed gain may come from candidate recall rather than reranking quality.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires search/retrieval domain knowledge about recall-vs-reranking decomposition.

planted_issues.description: The evidence supports a claim about the combined stack, not a clean claim that the new reranker itself is superior.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding of ablation requirements in ranking-system evaluation.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should separate end-to-end stack improvement from reranker-specific superiority and ask for a crossed ablation.
The segment gains for multilingual and tail queries are a clue that retrieval recall, not just reranking, may be driving the result.
Without evaluating both rerankers on both candidate pools, the memo cannot support the headline claim.
ideal_defense: The defense can reasonably say the business decision is whether to launch the combined stack, making an end-to-end claim defensible.
The defense should narrow the scientific claim and accept that the reranker-specific conclusion still needs an ablation matrix.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The old reranker on the new candidate pool captures most of the gain, showing the reranker-specific claim is overstated.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The new reranker beats the old reranker on both candidate pools, showing a genuine reranking advantage independent of retrieval.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Both components contribute materially, with neither ablation fully explaining the end-to-end gain.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a mechanically hard broken-baseline case because the correct resolution is mixed and requires a specific crossed ablation. A single-pass assessor will often jump to critique_wins without specifying the diagnostic test needed to distinguish retrieval and reranking effects.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 6, 8. Type A/D. The memo is self-consistent and the obvious unusual feature (offline replay) is a red herring. The real issue is an omitted ablation across interacting system changes. Likely failed dims: DRQ, ETD, IDR, and sometimes IDP.
notes: Hard BB case with two interacting high-severity issues and mixed empirical-test resolution.

task_prompt: AUDIT PREP MEMO — CUSTOMER SUPPORT TRIAGE MODEL REFRESH. The Support Operations analytics group proposes replacing the current ticket-routing model with a refreshed transformer classifier. The evaluation packet reports macro-F1 = 0.78 for the new model versus 0.69 for the incumbent baseline on a recent quarter of held-out tickets. Reviewers are told both systems were scored against the same gold labels from the quarterly quality-assurance audit. During the same quarter, however, the operations team consolidated three support queues into a single 'billing and account access' label after repeated escalation confusion. The new model was trained after the consolidation using the revised label map. The incumbent baseline was the production model trained under the old queue taxonomy and was not retrained because the legacy feature pipeline is being sunset. For scoring, the baseline's predictions under the old taxonomy were post-processed through a deterministic label crosswalk into the new QA label space. The memo describes this as a bookkeeping adjustment and argues that retraining the old model under the revised taxonomy would be wasted engineering effort because the baseline is being retired anyway. The report also notes that the biggest gain appears in precisely the merged billing/access category, where the new model reduces cross-queue confusion by 23%. Leadership quotes in the memo frame the result as strong evidence that the new architecture generalizes better to messy customer language and should become the default router before peak season. ground_truth.final_verdict: The baseline comparison is invalid because the challenger was trained after a label revision and the baseline was not retrained under the new label policy, so the models are being judged on different target definitions.

planted_issues.description: The challenger is trained on a revised target definition while the baseline remains trained on the old taxonomy, so the comparison does not isolate model quality under a common label policy.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that target-definition changes are part of the supervised learning problem and invalidate model-class comparisons if only one side is retrained.

planted_issues.description: A deterministic post-hoc label crosswalk does not recreate a model trained under the merged taxonomy; it preserves legacy decision boundaries that were learned for a different task.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires NLP/operations modeling knowledge about target-space changes and why remapping predictions is not equivalent to retraining.

planted_issues.description: The largest reported performance gain occurs in the merged class created by the policy change, which is exactly where the comparison is least informative about architecture quality.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing to inspect whether gains align with the changed label semantics rather than genuine model improvement.

ideal_critique: The study compares models across different target definitions; that alone invalidates the headline architecture claim.
Crosswalking legacy predictions into the new label map is not equivalent to retraining the legacy model under the new supervision policy.
The fact that the biggest gain sits in the relabeled queue is a strong clue that the policy revision, not just the new architecture, is driving the measured improvement.
ideal_defense: The defense can still argue the new end-to-end routing system is better for current operations, but it cannot use this study as a clean architecture comparison.
The defense should concede that a fair baseline would retrain the old model on the revised taxonomy.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Retraining the incumbent baseline on the revised taxonomy closes most of the macro-F1 gap, especially in the merged class.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Retraining the incumbent baseline on the revised taxonomy still leaves a similar performance gap across classes.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Retraining narrows the merged-class gap but some architecture advantage remains elsewhere.

ground_truth_rationale: The prompt looks like an ordinary support-NLP comparison, but the flaw is a hidden label-definition mismatch rather than an obvious baseline weakness. The justifications sound operationally sensible, which makes the case harder than a standard leakage example.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 3, 4, 6. Type C/D. No internal contradiction exists; the challenge is noticing that target revision changes the learning problem. Single-pass reviewers often fail IDR or misstate the issue as a generic class-imbalance concern (IDP).
notes: Hard BB case built around label-policy change plus wrong crosswalk justification.

task_prompt: A fraud detection team built a binary classifier on a dataset where 1% of transactions are fraudulent. They report 99.1% accuracy on a held-out test set and conclude that the model performs 'near perfectly.' No precision, recall, or F1 metrics are reported. The classifier was trained with balanced class weights. A closer look at the confusion matrix reveals that the model predicts 'not fraud' for every transaction and achieves 99% accuracy by never detecting a single case of fraud. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Accuracy is an inappropriate metric for this severely imbalanced fraud detection task; the model's apparent 99.1% accuracy is achieved by predicting the majority class for every example, detecting zero fraud cases.

planted_issues.description: Using accuracy on a 1% base rate task allows a null classifier (predict majority class always) to score 99%, masking complete failure on the minority class of interest.

ideal_critique: Accuracy on a 1% fraud rate is dominated by the majority class; recall, precision, and F1 or PR-AUC are required to assess fraud detection performance.
The confusion matrix reveals zero fraud cases detected, making the 99.1% figure misleading.
ideal_defense: The defense should concede accuracy is insufficient and propose precision-recall AUC as the primary evaluation metric for this class distribution.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Recall on the fraud class is 0 or near 0, confirming the model ignores the positive class.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Precision and recall on the fraud class are both high, indicating accuracy was misleadingly pessimistic framing.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Recall is non-zero but low (e.g., 40%).

ground_truth_rationale: The explicit mention of 1% fraud rate and 99.1% accuracy, combined with no detection, makes this the canonical accuracy-on-imbalanced-data mistake. Easy case.
notes: Classic easy metric mismatch. Null classifier on imbalanced data.

task_prompt: A summarization team evaluated their fine-tuned abstractive summarization model using ROUGE-L scores on the CNN/DailyMail benchmark. They achieved ROUGE-L = 0.412, beating the previous SOTA ROUGE-L = 0.389. They conclude that the model produces higher-quality summaries than prior systems. The evaluation was conducted without any human ratings. A human study was explicitly omitted because 'ROUGE-L correlates well with human judgments in prior work.' Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: ROUGE-L measures n-gram overlap rather than semantic quality; improvements in ROUGE-L do not necessarily correspond to improvements in human-judged summary quality, and the cited correlation with human judgment does not hold uniformly across model types.

planted_issues.description: ROUGE-L measures lexical overlap, not semantic accuracy or coherence; gains on ROUGE-L from abstractive models do not reliably translate to human-perceived quality improvements.

ideal_critique: ROUGE-L's correlation with human judgment holds mainly for extractive summarization; abstractive models can achieve high ROUGE-L with incoherent or factually incorrect outputs.
The omission of a human evaluation is the key gap; the ROUGE gain does not validate the claim of 'higher-quality summaries.'
ideal_defense: ROUGE-L is the standard benchmark metric; the comparison is internally consistent. Human evaluation is expensive and the team cited prior correlation evidence.
The defense might argue that ROUGE-L gains of this magnitude on CNN/DM do typically track human quality, making this a reasonable operating assumption.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A human evaluation of fluency, coherence, and faithfulness shows no improvement or a regression despite ROUGE-L gain.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A human evaluation confirms higher-quality summaries consistent with ROUGE-L improvement.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Human judges prefer the new model on fluency but not faithfulness.

ground_truth_rationale: ROUGE-vs-human validity is a known limitation explicitly omitted by the team with a weakly justified excuse. A single-pass reviewer will find this immediately.
notes: ROUGE vs human quality. Easy, well-known issue.

task_prompt: An e-commerce recommendation team trained a neural collaborative filtering model and measured its offline performance using NDCG@10. NDCG@10 improved from 0.32 (matrix factorization baseline) to 0.41 (neural CF), a 28% relative improvement. The team used a random 80/20 train/test split at the user-item interaction level. Based on this result, they launched the neural CF model in production, replacing the baseline for all users. Three months after deployment, an analysis of business metrics revealed that average order value declined by 4% and click-through rate on recommended items declined by 7%. The team is presenting this scenario for post-mortem review. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: NDCG@10 measured in random split offline evaluation does not account for popularity bias and exposure bias, leading to overestimation of real-world ranking quality; the offline-online correlation gap explains the business metric decline.

planted_issues.description: Random train/test splitting of user-item interactions inflates NDCG for models that memorize popularity patterns, since popular items appear in both train and test; temporal splits would better reflect online performance.
NDCG on held-out interactions measures retrieval of items users happened to interact with, not relevance quality; this metric has low correlation with online CTR or revenue lift in practice.
The launch decision is framed in business terms, but the evaluation reports only NDCG without showing that gains on this metric correspond to revenue or order-value outcomes in this catalog.

ideal_critique: Random interaction-level splits allow popular items to leak from train to test, making NDCG improvements reflect popularity memorization rather than personalization quality.
NDCG on random-split holdout has consistently poor correlation with online business metrics in recommendation systems; the business metric decline is the expected consequence.
ideal_defense: NDCG@10 is the industry-standard offline metric; the team followed standard practice.
Other factors (seasonality, product catalog changes) could explain the business metric decline independent of model quality.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A temporal split evaluation shows smaller or negative NDCG improvement for the neural CF model, suggesting random split overstated gains.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A temporal split evaluation replicates the 28% NDCG improvement, suggesting the offline metric was reliable.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A temporal split shows improvement, but online results still don't recover.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a post-mortem scenario with real evidence of failure. The two issues (split methodology and offline-online gap) each require connecting different facts. Medium difficulty.
notes: Recommender system offline-online gap. Medium.

task_prompt: A clinical NLP team developed a model to extract mentions of adverse drug reactions (ADRs) from clinical notes. They report micro-F1 = 0.86 across all entity types in their test set and recommend the model for production use in pharmacovigilance. The dataset contains 12 entity types with very different base rates: the most common type appears 4,200 times in the test set while the rarest appears 23 times. They note that rare entity types have lower F1 individually but contribute little to the micro-average. The pharmacovigilance team's primary concern is detecting rare, serious ADR signals. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Micro-F1 is dominated by frequent entity types and systematically understates performance (or failure) on rare entity types; for pharmacovigilance where rare serious ADRs are the primary concern, macro-F1 or per-class F1 on rare types is the appropriate metric.

planted_issues.description: Micro-F1 aggregates across all entity occurrences, giving 4200:1 weight to frequent vs. rare types; for pharmacovigilance focused on rare ADRs, this metric is insensitive to failure on the clinically critical rare classes.

ideal_critique: Micro-F1 weighted by entity frequency will be dominated by common entity types; the team acknowledges rare types perform worse but dismisses this as 'low contribution to micro-F1', which inverts the clinical priority.
Pharmacovigilance requires high recall on rare serious ADRs; macro-F1 or per-class recall on rare types must be the primary metric.
ideal_defense: Micro-F1 = 0.86 does reflect overall extraction quality; the team could augment with per-class metrics rather than replacing the metric entirely.
The model can be useful for frequent entity types even if rare ADR detection requires further work.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Per-class F1 on the rarest entity types is below 0.5, confirming that micro-F1 masked critical failures.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Per-class F1 on rare types is comparable to frequent types, showing micro-F1 is representative.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Rare class performance is moderate (0.6–0.7), not clearly sufficient or insufficient for pharmacovigilance.

ground_truth_rationale: The scenario explicitly states rare types perform worse but argues they 'contribute little to micro-F1', which is precisely the flaw: the metric choice inverts the clinical priority. Requires connecting the micro-F1 property with the pharmacovigilance context.
notes: Micro vs macro F1 in clinical NLP. Medium: requires connecting metric property to use case priority.

task_prompt: A content moderation team trained a toxicity classifier and reports that its overall precision and recall are both above 0.80. They used a balanced test set (equal positive and negative examples) for evaluation. The production environment has a 2% toxicity rate in real traffic. The team sets the decision threshold at 0.50 for production deployment. They argue that since precision and recall are both high on the balanced test set, performance in production should be similar. A compliance officer asks whether the false positive rate has been characterized. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Precision and recall measured on a balanced test set cannot be extrapolated to production where the toxicity base rate is 2%; at this base rate, even a high-precision classifier will generate a large absolute number of false positives, and the true positive predictive value will be substantially lower than the balanced-set precision.

planted_issues.description: Metrics from a balanced evaluation set (50% positive) do not apply to production with 2% base rate; PPV (precision) degrades substantially at low prevalence even with fixed sensitivity/specificity.

ideal_critique: At 2% base rate, even a model with 80% precision on a balanced set will have much lower positive predictive value (PPV) in production; the evaluation methodology does not reflect the deployment distribution.
The Bayesian base-rate shift means the team cannot claim production performance will match balanced-test-set metrics.
ideal_defense: The model can be recalibrated or the threshold adjusted for the production distribution; the balanced test set gives a good read on the model's intrinsic discrimination ability.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Recomputing precision at 2% base rate (via Bayes' theorem or held-out production sample) shows PPV below 0.50.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A held-out production sample maintains precision above 0.80 at the 0.50 threshold.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: PPV is between 0.50 and 0.80, insufficient but not catastrophic.

ground_truth_rationale: The 50% balanced test set and 2% production rate are both stated; connecting them requires understanding Bayesian base-rate effects. Medium difficulty: requires a calculation or at minimum recognizing the distribution shift.
notes: Base rate shift from balanced evaluation. Medium: requires connecting stated facts.

task_prompt:
LAUNCH PROPOSAL: NEXUS PERSONALIZATION ENGINE — READY FOR FULL DEPLOYMENT

To: VP Product, Chief Data Officer
From: Applied ML Team (Recommender Systems)
Date: October 15

After 14 months of development and a rigorous offline evaluation program, the Applied ML team recommends immediate transition to full deployment of the Nexus personalization engine across all content verticals.

The core evaluation methodology follows industry-standard offline benchmarking practice. We constructed a time-stratified holdout spanning a 60-day window, retaining the most recent 20% of user interactions as the test set across all verticals. This temporal holdout correctly avoids future leakage. On this holdout, Nexus achieves NDCG@20 = 0.438 versus 0.387 for the production ranker — a 13.2% relative improvement. The bootstrap 95% CI on the relative improvement is [+9.8%, +16.7%], computed over 1,000 resamples of user sessions.

The team has high confidence in this result. The NDCG@20 improvement is consistent across content verticals (Sports: +11.4%, Entertainment: +14.8%, News: +12.9%, Lifestyle: +13.7%) and across user tenure segments (0–90 days: +12.1%, 91–365 days: +13.8%, 365+ days: +13.0%). This consistency rules out the possibility of localized overfitting to a particular user segment or content type.

The team emphasizes that the evaluation was designed specifically to address the offline-to-online correlation concerns raised in the last recommendation system review. Our approach followed the calibrated offline evaluation methodology described in our internal research memo (Applied ML Research Memo #142, June): specifically, we applied inverse propensity weighting (IPW) to the NDCG calculation, using propensity scores estimated from the production ranker's logging policy. This debiases the standard replay-based evaluation by upweighting underexposed items that would have been exposed under Nexus but were not under the production ranker.

IPW-NDCG has been shown in the literature to improve offline-to-online correlation relative to unweighted NDCG. We believe this gives us strong grounds for deployment confidence.

Implementation timeline: Engineering is ready. Infrastructure provisioning will begin November 1st. Full rollout across all content verticals completes by November 28th. Q4 business impact is estimated at +$4.2M incremental engagement revenue based on our historical lift-to-revenue model. The appendix to the memo includes routine implementation details — staffing plans, rollout checkpoints, dashboard ownership, and escalation pathways — because the organization is preparing for deployment rather than for an academic publication. Those details are deliberately realistic but methodologically secondary. They create the texture of a credible internal review and force the reader to distinguish operational confidence from actual evidentiary support for the claim. Those details are deliberately realistic but methodologically secondary. They create the texture of a credible internal review and force the reader to distinguish operational confidence from actual evidentiary support for the claim. The appendix to the memo includes routine implementation details — staffing plans, rollout checkpoints, dashboard ownership, and escalation pathways — because the organization is preparing for deployment rather than for an academic publication. Those details are deliberately realistic but methodologically secondary. They create the texture of a credible internal review and force the reader to distinguish operational confidence from actual evidentiary support for the claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: IPW-NDCG corrects for exposure bias but only works when the propensity model is correctly specified; propensity scores estimated from the logging policy are unreliable for items rarely or never shown under that policy (positivity violation), and IPW corrections using estimated (not true) propensities introduce bias that is not disclosed.

planted_issues.description: IPW-based debiasing requires overlap/positivity (every item has nonzero probability of being shown under the logging policy); novel Nexus recommendations not present under the production ranker have propensity scores near zero, causing IPW weights to blow up and making the NDCG estimate highly unstable or undefined for novel items.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowledge of the positivity assumption in off-policy evaluation and its violation in recommender system settings where new models recommend novel items.

planted_issues.description: Using estimated propensity scores (from the logging policy) rather than true propensities introduces additional variance and bias into IPW-NDCG; the standard IPW consistency guarantee assumes known propensities, not estimated ones.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowledge of the distinction between known and estimated propensities in causal off-policy evaluation; known in causal inference literature but not broadly known in applied ML.

planted_issues.description: If the team clips extreme IPW weights to control variance, the estimator becomes biased; failing to disclose clipping or truncation policy makes the reported CI uninterpretable.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires off-policy evaluation knowledge about clipping-induced bias.

ideal_critique: IPW-based offline evaluation requires that the logging policy has nonzero probability for all items the new policy might recommend; items that Nexus recommends but the production ranker never showed have estimated propensities near zero, causing IPW weights to diverge.
The team applies IPW without disclosing how it handles near-zero propensities; if these are clipped (common practice), the NDCG is still biased; if not clipped, the estimator has high variance.
Estimated propensity scores from the logging policy are not the same as true propensities; IPW with estimated propensities has larger bias than acknowledged.
ideal_defense: The IPW correction is a meaningful improvement over unweighted NDCG; the baseline positivity concern is acknowledged in the research memo but the team believes Nexus and production ranker share sufficient overlap.
Propensity clipping is a standard variance-reduction technique; if applied, it should be disclosed.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: An online A/B test shows the actual lift is below the IPW-NDCG lower CI bound of +9.8%, or shows no statistically significant improvement.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: An online A/B test confirms lift within [+9.8%, +16.7%], validating the IPW-NDCG estimate.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Online lift is positive but below the lower CI bound, indicating the IPW correction overstated but did not entirely fabricate the improvement.

ground_truth_rationale: The IPW-NDCG correction is presented as a solution to the offline-online correlation problem, but its validity depends on the positivity assumption and known propensities — neither of which is verified. The document is internally consistent and the justification ('IPW-NDCG improves offline-online correlation') is correct in principle but subtly wrong in this application.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 6, 8. Flaw type A (positivity assumption violation in off-policy evaluation). The obvious red herring: bootstrap CI validity or temporal holdout methodology — both of which are actually sound. The real flaw is in the positivity assumption for the IPW correction, which requires causal inference / off-policy evaluation knowledge to identify. IDR: reviewers without off-policy evaluation knowledge will miss ipw_positivity_violation. IDP: reviewers who flag temporal holdout are wrong. DC: the verdict requires understanding why IPW fails here, not just flagging 'IPW can be wrong.' ETD: the test is an online A/B test, which is the only way to validate off-policy estimates. FVC: the NDCG improvement may not translate online, but the mechanism requires domain knowledge.
notes: Hard case. IPW-NDCG positivity violation in recommender systems. Off-policy evaluation domain knowledge required. Red herring: temporal holdout and bootstrap CI (both valid). Must not claim: temporal holdout is wrong, bootstrap is wrong, vertical consistency is suspicious.

task_prompt: LEGAL AI QUALITY REVIEW — EXECUTIVE SUMMARY. The memo argues this metric specifically addresses the historical criticism that overlap-based scores miss hallucinated statements. Those checks all passed and are presented as reassurance to stakeholders, which makes the remaining methodological flaw easy to miss on a quick read. Stakeholder comments in the appendix describe the result as strong enough for a phased rollout, but do not add new evidence. This operational confidence is part of the scenario noise: the methodology sounds polished and complete, and the critical issue is in an assumption or omission that is not obvious from surface-level review. The writing tone is intentionally confident because the project has already passed infrastructure review and the remaining question is whether the evidence supports the claimed methodological conclusion. ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported win on automated faithfulness metrics is not sufficient to claim higher legal-summary quality, but the study may still justify the model for the narrow scope of triage drafting if a targeted human evaluation confirms risk-sensitive dimensions.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a blind attorney review focused on omission of material holdings, hallucinated obligations, and citation correctness on the same held-out matter set.

planted_issues.description: The composite metric measures lexical and sentence-level support, but legal-summary quality depends on omission of material holdings, exceptions, and obligations that can be absent without strongly affecting the automatic score.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires legal-document summarization knowledge that materiality is not captured by generic overlap or entailment metrics.

planted_issues.description: The memo claims higher-quality legal summaries overall, but the evidence only supports a narrower operational claim about first-pass intake drafting and even that claim needs targeted human review on risk-sensitive dimensions.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding of metric validity relative to narrow vs broad task scope.

planted_issues.description: Prior internal calibration of the entailment metric does not establish validity for this mix of legal matter types, where omission and clause interaction can dominate quality judgments.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires domain knowledge about legal NLP evaluation transferability.

ideal_critique: A calibrated critique should not simply say ROUGE is bad; it should explain why legally material omissions and clause interactions escape the chosen metric stack.
The presence of the source file beside the summary mitigates but does not eliminate the risk, so the correct verdict depends on the exact operational scope.
The evidence could support a narrow triage-drafting use if targeted human review on legal-risk dimensions confirms no regression.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue that for first-pass intake, summaries are assistive and always reviewed with source documents visible, making automatic metrics a reasonable initial filter.
The defense should narrow the broad headline claim and accept that attorney evaluation is still required before asserting higher overall legal-summary quality.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Blind attorney review finds more material omissions or citation-context errors in the new model despite better automatic scores.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Blind attorney review rates the new model equal or better on materiality, citation correctness, and hallucination risk.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Attorney review shows gains on drafting speed but mixed results on materiality-sensitive cases.

ground_truth_rationale: This metric-mismatch case is intentionally domain-specific: the issue is not that automatic metrics are useless, but that they do not validate legal materiality for the broad quality claim the memo makes. The defense has a genuine narrow-scope argument, making the correct position mixed.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 6, 8. Type A/D. The obvious feature (ROUGE in legal NLP) is a red herring because the memo includes a better faithfulness metric. The hard part is noticing what even the improved metric stack still misses. Likely failures: IDR, DRQ, ETD, IDP.
notes: Hard MM case requiring legal NLP evaluation knowledge.

task_prompt: RISK COMMITTEE BRIEFING — FAIRNESS REVIEW OF CARD FRAUD MODEL. The Payments ML team is presenting the annual fairness review of the card-not-present fraud detector. The report opens positively: false-decline complaints are down year over year and the new detector maintains strong precision at the current block threshold. The fairness section highlights near-parity on equalized odds across age bands and geographic regions when evaluated on the last four quarters of confirmed fraud outcomes. This parity result is used to support the statement that the model is fair in deployment. The outcome label used for fairness analysis is confirmed fraud after case investigation or chargeback. Transactions not escalated for investigation and transactions where the customer never disputes the charge remain labeled non-fraud. The operations appendix notes that investigation capacity is prioritized for high-dollar and cross-border transactions, and that domestic low-dollar alerts are frequently auto-cleared because analysts focus on queues with higher expected recovery value. The memo says this is acceptable because fairness should be assessed on reliable labels, and confirmed-fraud is the cleanest label available. The same memo also reports that customer reimbursement complaints are disproportionately concentrated in younger cardholders even though equalized-odds parity on confirmed fraud is close to zero difference. Product leadership cites the parity chart as evidence the system can safely be expanded to more aggressive auto-decline thresholds next quarter. Operational details were included in the review packet because the program is already resourced for launch: engineering capacity is allocated for the next release train, operations has staffed the monitoring rota, and finance has provisionally reserved budget contingent on a methods sign-off. The memo circulated among the data science review board, product leadership, and domain stakeholders. The writing tone is intentionally confident because the project has already passed infrastructure review and the remaining question is whether the evidence supports the claimed methodological conclusion. The document also notes that the team reviewed obvious concerns from prior launches: data source continuity, test environment stability, and reporting consistency. Those checks all passed and are presented as reassurance to stakeholders, which makes the remaining methodological flaw easy to miss on a quick read. Stakeholder comments in the appendix describe the result as strong enough for a phased rollout, but do not add new evidence. This operational confidence is part of the scenario noise: the methodology sounds polished and complete, and the critical issue is in an assumption or omission that is not obvious from surface-level review.
