ground_truth.final_verdict: The fairness claim is unsupported because equalized-odds parity on confirmed-fraud labels does not measure the business harm the system creates under delayed and selective fraud confirmation.

planted_issues.description: Equalized odds is computed on confirmed fraud labels that are themselves selectively observed through investigation and chargeback processes, so parity on those labels does not reflect parity in true fraud detection or customer harm.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires AML/fraud operations knowledge about delayed and selective ground-truth ascertainment.

planted_issues.description: The committee uses a parity metric on confirmed fraud while the deployment harm includes false declines and reimbursement burden; the fairness metric is mismatched to the customer-harm claim.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that fairness evaluation must match the operational harm being controlled, not just the easiest label.

planted_issues.description: Because analysts prioritize high-dollar and cross-border alerts, certain groups and transaction types are more likely to receive confirmed labels, embedding operational triage bias into the fairness target.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires fraud-ops knowledge about investigation queues as a label-generation mechanism.

ideal_critique: A correct critique must go beyond saying equalized odds is imperfect; it must explain that the label itself is a function of investigation policy and customer dispute behavior.
The reimbursement complaint disparity is a clue that the fairness target in the memo does not align with the actual customer-harm objective.
Expanding to more aggressive auto-decline thresholds on the basis of this fairness evidence is not justified.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue confirmed fraud is the cleanest available operational label, but that only justifies using it for one narrow analysis, not for the broad fairness-in-deployment claim.
The defense should propose complementary fairness audits on reimbursement complaints, false declines, and dispute burden.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A shadow audit using a stratified random sample of auto-cleared transactions reveals group differences in latent fraud and false-decline burden not visible in confirmed-fraud parity.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A stratified audit finds that confirmed-fraud parity closely tracks parity in reimbursement burden and false declines.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Some group differences appear in customer-harm metrics but are operationally small relative to the overall complaint decline.

ground_truth_rationale: This case is hard because the metric itself looks sophisticated and fairness-specific. The real problem is the label-generation process and the mismatch between confirmed-fraud parity and customer-harm fairness.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 3, 4, 8. Type A/C/D. Single-pass reviewers may stop at the obvious fairness metric and miss that the target is operationally biased. Likely failed dims: IDR, DC, IDP.
notes: Hard MM case requiring fraud-operations label provenance knowledge without using a simple leakage pattern.

task_prompt: A retail chain deployed a new product placement algorithm in 200 stores starting in November. Sales of the featured products increased by 18% compared to the same 200 stores in October. The team concludes the algorithm caused a significant sales lift and recommends expanding to all 1,200 stores. No control stores were used in the analysis. The evaluation period spans the pre-holiday and holiday shopping seasons. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The sales increase is confounded by seasonal effects (November–December holiday shopping season) since no control stores were included and the comparison period is October vs November–December.

planted_issues.description: The October-to-November comparison spans the transition into holiday shopping season; without control stores, seasonal sales increases are indistinguishable from algorithm effects.

ideal_critique: October-to-November retail typically shows organic sales increases due to seasonal shopping patterns; the absence of a control group makes it impossible to attribute the 18% lift to the algorithm.
A proper evaluation requires a concurrent control group of comparable stores not using the algorithm.
ideal_defense: Defense might note that an 18% lift exceeds typical seasonal effects, but without controls, this claim cannot be substantiated.
The defense should propose a controlled A/B test with matched stores as the remediation.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Control stores without the algorithm show comparable or larger sales increases over the same period.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Control stores without the algorithm show flat or negative sales over the same period, supporting algorithm attribution.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Control stores show some increase (e.g., 10%) but smaller than treated stores (18%).

ground_truth_rationale: The seasonal confound and absence of controls are both explicitly stated. This is a directly readable, single-signal flaw.
notes: Classic no-control-group seasonal confound. Easy.

task_prompt: A health app company analyzed whether users who engaged with their guided meditation feature had lower reported stress levels than users who did not. Among 10,000 users, those who used the meditation feature at least 3 times per week reported stress levels 2.1 points lower (on a 10-point scale) compared to non-users. The company concludes the meditation feature reduces stress by 2.1 points on average and promotes the feature in their marketing as clinically beneficial. Engagement with the feature was entirely voluntary. No randomization was used. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The analysis is subject to self-selection bias: users who voluntarily engage with meditation features are likely already more motivated to reduce stress and may have lower baseline stress, confounding the observed difference.

planted_issues.description: Voluntary meditation feature engagement is strongly correlated with pre-existing motivation to reduce stress, making the stress difference attributable to self-selection rather than the feature's causal effect.

ideal_critique: Self-selection makes users who choose to meditate systematically different from non-users in ways correlated with stress; observed differences cannot be attributed to the feature.
The 'clinically beneficial' marketing claim requires causal evidence from a randomized trial, not observational data.
ideal_defense: The correlation is real even if causality is uncertain; the company could frame the marketing as 'associated with' rather than 'causes.'
A propensity score analysis on baseline characteristics could partially address the self-selection concern.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Users who engaged and non-users differ significantly on baseline stress scores measured at account creation, confirming selection into the feature is not random.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Baseline stress scores are comparable across meditation users and non-users, suggesting selection on stress severity is not a major confounder.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Baseline scores differ slightly but propensity-matched comparison still shows a significant effect.

ground_truth_rationale: Voluntary engagement = self-selection bias is directly inferable from the scenario. Easy single-signal case.
notes: Self-selection in voluntary digital health engagement. Easy.

task_prompt: A digital marketing team ran a campaign optimization study. They trained a propensity model to assign users to email campaign variants (A or B) and measured 30-day conversion rates. Variant A achieved 5.2% and Variant B 4.1% conversion. The propensity model balanced observed covariates (age, region, purchase history). The team claims that after propensity weighting, the groups are comparable and the difference is causal. The study was conducted during a period when the company was simultaneously running television advertisements that reached different geographic regions with different intensity — some regions had heavy TV ad coverage, others had none. The propensity model did not include TV ad exposure as a covariate because it was considered a company-level intervention, not a user-level confounder. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: TV ad exposure is a user-level confounder (varies by region and thus by user) that was excluded from the propensity model; if TV ad intensity is correlated with region and also affects conversion, the propensity model does not achieve exchangeability.

planted_issues.description: TV ad exposure varies by region and affects conversion probability; because the propensity model conditions on region but not TV intensity directly, users in high-TV regions assigned to Variant A may be more likely to convert regardless of variant.

Propensity score methods only achieve causal identification under the unconfoundedness assumption (all confounders observed); the omitted TV exposure variable violates this assumption if it independently affects both variant assignment (through regional targeting) and conversion.
Because TV campaigns spill over geographically and socially, the user-level treatment assumption is violated; regional media exposure can affect untreated users in adjacent markets as well.

ideal_critique: TV ad intensity varies by region and affects conversion; if Variant A was also more targeted toward high-TV regions, the conversion difference is confounded.
Propensity score methods require all confounders to be in the propensity model; excluding TV exposure as 'company-level' doesn't prevent it from acting as a user-level confounder.
ideal_defense: If TV ad exposure was randomly distributed across both variants within regions, it would not confound the comparison.
The team could check whether Variant A and B had similar regional distributions; if so, TV confounding is unlikely.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Variant A is over-represented in high-TV-exposure regions compared to Variant B.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: TV ad exposure is balanced across Variant A and Variant B users after propensity weighting.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Regional TV exposure imbalance exists but is small relative to the conversion gap.

ground_truth_rationale: The TV ad confound is present in the scenario but not labeled as a confounder. Connecting the 'company-level' framing with the user-level impact requires inferring that regional TV exposure creates user-level variation in conversion probability. Medium difficulty.
notes: Omitted regional confounder in propensity study. Medium.

task_prompt: A healthcare analytics firm evaluated a readmission prediction model across 8 hospitals. They report macro-averaged AUC = 0.76 and note performance is consistent across hospitals (range: 0.73–0.79). The model was trained on 2 years of data from all 8 hospitals combined. One of the 8 hospitals (Hospital G) implemented a new electronic health records system midway through the training window. The team notes this in passing but argues the hospital-consistent AUC suggests no major data quality issues. Hospital G's patients account for 31% of the training data. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Hospital G's EHR transition creates temporal feature inconsistency: pre-transition records likely have different missingness patterns and coding conventions than post-transition records; training the model on combined data from both periods without adjustment introduces a data quality confounder that may inflate or distort AUC estimates.

planted_issues.description: Hospital G's EHR system change mid-training-window creates a structural break in feature distributions (coding, missingness, documentation practices); training on combined pre/post-transition data introduces a temporal feature confounder that artificially biases the model.

ideal_critique: An EHR system transition changes which features are recorded, their coding conventions, and missingness patterns; combining pre- and post-transition data for 31% of the training population creates a spurious temporal feature that the model may learn.
Consistent AUC across hospitals does not rule out feature confounding — the feature shift exists within Hospital G, not between hospitals.
ideal_defense: The consistent AUC range (0.73–0.79) across all hospitals including G suggests the model is not severely disrupted.
EHR transitions are common in healthcare data; if the team stratified by pre/post transition within G and found similar performance, the concern would be addressed.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Stratifying Hospital G's data by pre/post EHR transition reveals different feature distributions and different model performance in each period.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Pre- and post-transition records from Hospital G show comparable feature distributions and AUC, suggesting the transition had minimal impact on model features.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Feature distributions differ but AUC is similar, suggesting the model is robust to the feature shift.

ground_truth_rationale: EHR transition is mentioned but not labeled as a confounder; connecting midway EHR transition to feature distribution changes requires healthcare ML domain knowledge. Medium.
notes: EHR transition creates within-hospital temporal feature confound. Medium.

task_prompt:
INTERNAL MEMO — MODEL GOVERNANCE SUBMISSION
Subject: ProactiveRisk v2.0 — Clinical Risk Model Validation Summary
Prepared by: Clinical Analytics Team | Review Date: March 4

The ProactiveRisk v2.0 model predicts 90-day hospitalization risk for patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) in our integrated care network. This document summarizes the validation analysis supporting deployment.

Feature set and training: The model uses 84 features including lab values (eGFR, creatinine, potassium, phosphorus), medication adherence signals, care gap indicators, and encounter frequency over rolling 12-month windows. Features are extracted from the electronic health record as of the index date for each patient-quarter. Training data covers 2019–2022 across 14 clinics and 18,400 patient-quarters. The held-out test set covers Q1–Q2 2023 (4,200 patient-quarters from 2,900 unique patients).

Model performance: The model achieves AUC = 0.83 on the held-out test set, with calibration confirmed by a Hosmer-Lemeshow test (p=0.41, indicating no significant miscalibration). At the operational threshold of 0.18 (chosen to match care coordinator capacity), sensitivity = 0.74 and specificity = 0.81.

Addressing known risks: The team reviewed standard risks for clinical risk model validation. Patient overlap between training and test sets is prevented by a patient-level temporal split: no patient appears in both the training cohort and the Q1–Q2 2023 test set. Secular trends in CKD documentation and coding practices were assessed; ICD coding audits show no significant changes in CKD severity staging criteria between 2019 and 2023. Demographic parity was verified: AUC by race/ethnicity shows no gaps greater than 0.04. Missing data is handled via median imputation for continuous features and modal imputation for categoricals, consistent with prior model generations.

The care coordination team (Sunita Rajan, Director) has confirmed operational readiness for April 1st deployment, with model scores integrated into the care management dashboard. Quarterly monitoring reports will track observed hospitalization rates against predicted rates. The appendix to the memo includes routine implementation details — staffing plans, rollout checkpoints, dashboard ownership, and escalation pathways — because the organization is preparing for deployment rather than for an academic publication. Those details are deliberately realistic but methodologically secondary. They create the texture of a credible internal review and force the reader to distinguish operational confidence from actual evidentiary support for the claim. ground_truth.final_verdict: The model uses medication adherence signals and care gap indicators as features, but these are recorded after the patient's care trajectory has already begun; no disclosure is provided about whether these features are computed strictly before the hospitalization outcome could influence them, creating a potential temporal leakage that is unaddressed in the validation.

planted_issues.description: Medication adherence and care gap features may reflect patient behavior that occurs after the early signals of the hospitalization being predicted; without explicit documentation that all features are computed strictly prior to any outcome-related care escalation, temporal leakage cannot be ruled out for these dynamic features.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowledge that medication adherence signals in EHR systems often reflect dispensing records that can be updated retroactively or that care gaps can be closed by hospitalization-related encounters themselves; clinical ML requires explicit temporal ordering documentation for dynamic features.

planted_issues.description: Median imputation is applied without assessing whether missingness is informative (MNAR) in this CKD population; missing lab values in CKD patients often predict deterioration (tests ordered less frequently when patients disengage from care), making median imputation systematically biased.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowledge that in chronic disease management, missing lab values are often missing-not-at-random (MNAR): patients who miss labs are precisely those at higher risk of poor outcomes; median imputation will underestimate risk for these patients.

planted_issues.description: Quarterly feature refreshes in integrated care systems often backfill encounter-linked adherence or care-gap fields after discharge-related events, so feature timestamp policy must be explicit to rule out silent refresh leakage.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires EHR operations knowledge about retrospective field refresh and care-gap closure logic.

ideal_critique: The validation summary addresses standard risks (patient overlap, coding drift, demographic parity) but does not document the temporal ordering of feature computation relative to outcome-relevant care escalations; for dynamic features like medication adherence and care gaps, this omission is material.
Median imputation without MNAR assessment is a critical omission in a CKD cohort where lab missingness is strongly correlated with care disengagement, which in turn predicts hospitalization.
ideal_defense: The temporal split prevents patient-level leakage; individual feature-level temporal ordering is a separate concern that may be addressed in the feature engineering documentation.
Imputation methodology is consistent with prior model generations; this continuity provides a comparison baseline.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Feature importance analysis shows medication adherence is among the top predictors, and an audit reveals adherence records can be updated after hospitalization-related pharmacy encounters.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Feature extraction timestamps confirm all features are extracted strictly 30+ days before the hospitalization outcome window, ruling out temporal leakage.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Feature timestamps are verified as prior to the hospitalization window but the MNAR concern for lab missingness remains unaddressed.

ground_truth_rationale: The validation addresses the standard checklist items (patient overlap, coding drift, fairness) but omits temporal ordering documentation for dynamic features and MNAR assessment. Both omissions are invisible to consistency checking — the document is internally consistent, there is nothing to contradict.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 3, 8. Flaw type C (critical omissions: temporal ordering + MNAR). The document methodically addresses the standard confound checklist (patient overlap, coding drift, fairness), defeating the Observational Study Confound Enumeration strategy. The real flaws are absences: no temporal ordering documentation for dynamic features, no MNAR assessment for labs. These require knowing what SHOULD be in a clinical risk model validation — not checking stated facts against each other. IDR: reviewers without clinical ML standards knowledge will miss both omissions. IDP: ICD coding drift (explicitly addressed) and patient-level split (correct) are red herrings. DC: reviewer must know the clinical ML validation standard to notice what's missing. ETD: feature timestamp audit is the definitive test. DRQ: mixed/empirical_test because temporal leakage could go either way pending audit.
notes: Hard case. Clinical risk model validation with omitted temporal ordering and MNAR. Standard checklist items are all addressed (red herrings). Must not claim: AUC suspicious, patient split wrong, ICD coding unaddressed.

task_prompt: OPERATIONS REVIEW — SURGICAL SCHEDULING OPTIMIZER. ground_truth.final_verdict: The observed efficiency gain may reflect the optimization tool, but the evidence is confounded by clinician-led case sequencing and ward-level staffing changes that activate alongside the tool; a controlled stepped-wedge analysis is needed before attributing the full effect.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compare wards entering the scheduler at different pre-specified dates using a stepped-wedge design while controlling for staffing ratios and elective-case mix.

planted_issues.description: The optimizer launch coincides with a morning huddle process, centralized anesthesia float support, and block-time reallocation, so the measured improvement cannot be uniquely attributed to the scheduling model.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires perioperative operations knowledge about how staffing and block allocation independently affect OR throughput.

planted_issues.description: Pilot sites may have received disproportionate float-pool support during the rollout, creating an unmeasured concurrent intervention that biases the comparison with non-pilot hospitals.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires hospital operations knowledge about staffing interventions during pilot implementations.

planted_issues.description: The largest gains occur in the service line that also experienced block-time changes, which is a plausible non-model explanation for the measured reduction in overruns.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding of how elective-case mix and block shifts change OR timing metrics independent of a predictive scheduler.

ideal_critique: The memo embeds the model inside a workflow bundle, so the efficiency gain is not identified as a pure model effect.
Comparing pilot to non-pilot sites is not enough if staffing support and service-line mix changed differentially at pilot sites.
The correct resolution is a stepped-wedge or crossed implementation analysis, not a blanket rejection of the tool.
ideal_defense: The defense can plausibly argue that real-world OR optimization is inherently socio-technical and that end-to-end bundle performance matters for deployment.
But if the memo wants to claim the model itself caused the measured gain, it needs a design that separates the workflow bundle from the predictive component.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A stepped-wedge analysis shows wards receiving the workflow changes without the optimizer improve similarly, or gains vanish after controlling for staffing and case mix.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Hospitals entering the optimizer at different dates improve only after the model launch and not after staffing/case-mix controls are applied.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model appears beneficial, but the bundle still explains part of the observed effect.

ground_truth_rationale: This hidden-confounding case is hard because the memo anticipates simple controls and appears to address them. The real problem is an interacting bundle of operational changes that travel with the optimizer.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 6, 8. Type A/C/D. The first-pass read sounds plausible because non-pilot sites are used as controls, but domain knowledge is needed to see that staffing support and block-time shifts create nonstandard confounding. Likely failed dims: IDR, DRQ, ETD, DC.
notes: Hard HC case with multiple interacting high-severity operational confounders.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The measured treatment effect is confounded by network interference: the recommendation experiment assumes user independence, but the outcome is social adoption that propagates through the same graph used for targeting.

planted_issues.description: User-level randomization assumes one user’s treatment does not affect another user’s outcome, but follow behavior propagates through activity feeds and graph neighborhoods, violating SUTVA.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires network-experiment knowledge about interference in social systems.

planted_issues.description: Daily refresh of popularity and graph-neighborhood features allows treated-user behavior to enter the feature space used for control-user recommendations during the measurement window.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires recommender/social-graph experimentation knowledge.

planted_issues.description: Capping treatment at 50% reduces but does not remove spillover; once treatment and control users remain on the same graph, interference remains a first-order concern.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding of exposure mapping and partial-interference assumptions in network experiments.

ideal_critique: The experiment sounds randomized and large, but the real flaw is interference on the social graph, not sampling error.
Daily graph-feature refresh is especially problematic because treatment effects can leak directly into control recommendations.
The 50% cap is a plausible but insufficient justification; partial treatment does not solve SUTVA violation when treated and control users interact.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue the business question concerns ecosystem-wide effect, in which case the measured result may still be directionally useful.
But for a user-level causal estimate, cluster randomization or network-aware analysis is needed.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A cluster-randomized rerun on graph communities shows a substantially different treatment effect than the user-level experiment.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A graph-cluster rerun yields a similar effect size, suggesting spillover was modest.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Cluster randomization reduces the effect size but does not eliminate it.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a hidden-confounding case where standard A/B-test cues make the study feel safe. The flaw is a network-effect assumption violation that cannot be found by searching for contradictory facts inside the memo.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 3, 4, 8. Type A/C/D. Likely single-pass failures: IDR (missing interference), IDP (raising generic imbalance complaints), and DC.
notes: Hard HC case requiring network experimentation knowledge.

task_prompt: A hospital system trained a model to predict which patients are at high risk of a 30-day readmission. The model achieved AUC = 0.79 on a held-out test set. The hospital's care coordination team plans to use the model to reduce readmissions by proactively intervening with high-risk patients — calling them, arranging follow-up visits, and connecting them to social services. The team concludes that deploying this predictive model will reduce readmissions by the amount implied by the AUC lift over random selection. No randomized trial has been conducted to measure whether interventions change readmission rates. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model predicts readmission risk but cannot support causal claims about intervention effects; high-risk patients may be readmitted regardless of intervention, and the AUC cannot be translated into readmission reduction without an RCT.

planted_issues.description: Predicting which patients are high-risk does not establish that intervening on those patients will reduce readmissions; intervening on predictions requires causal evidence about intervention efficacy.

ideal_critique: A predictive model does not have causal effects; high-risk patients may be readmitted due to causes that interventions cannot address.
The AUC measures discrimination (ranking), not intervention effect size; no conversion between AUC and readmission reduction rate is valid without an RCT.
ideal_defense: Targeting interventions at high-risk patients is a reasonable application of predictive models even without a completed RCT.
The defense should propose a pilot RCT to measure actual intervention effectiveness.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: An RCT shows that intervening on high-risk flagged patients does not reduce readmissions at the predicted rate.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: An RCT shows that high-risk patients who receive intervention have lower readmissions than randomized controls.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Intervention helps some patient subgroups but not others.

ground_truth_rationale: Prediction-vs-intervention conflation is directly stated: 'the team concludes deploying this predictive model will reduce readmissions.' The AUC-to-reduction-rate translation is explicitly claimed. Easy single-signal case.
notes: Classic prediction vs intervention conflation in clinical ML. Easy.

task_prompt: An HR analytics team built a model to predict employee flight risk (whether an employee will leave within 6 months). The model achieves AUC = 0.77 on a held-out test set from 12 months of historical data. The team proposes using the model scores to proactively offer retention bonuses to high-risk employees. They estimate that if 60% of flagged employees can be retained through bonuses, the cost savings from reduced turnover would justify the investment. The 60% retention rate estimate is derived from the overall voluntary retention rate across all employees who received any retention bonus in the prior year — regardless of whether those bonuses were given to high-risk or low-risk employees. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The 60% retention rate used to justify the ROI is derived from all bonus recipients in the prior year, not from high-risk employees; high-risk employees may be less responsive to bonuses, making the projected ROI invalid.

planted_issues.description: The 60% projected retention rate is from all bonus recipients (likely a mix of low and high flight-risk employees), not specifically from high-risk employees who are the target of the proposed intervention.

The model predicts leaving intention, not responsiveness to interventions; high-risk employees may be leaving for reasons that bonuses cannot address (e.g., career trajectory, management conflict), making the 60% retention assumption overstated.
The ROI projection assumes homogeneous treatment effect of bonuses across all flagged employees, but the model predicts leaving risk rather than responsiveness to incentives.

ideal_critique: The 60% retention rate estimate is drawn from a population that includes low-risk employees who received routine retention bonuses; applying this rate to high-risk employees assumes equal responsiveness, which is unsubstantiated.
Flight risk prediction does not imply that the risk is addressable by bonuses; reasons for leaving may be non-monetary.
ideal_defense: The 60% rate is an aggregate and may be conservative if high-risk employees are particularly money-motivated.
The model still provides value by prioritizing which employees to invest retention efforts in, even if the ROI estimate needs refinement.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A pilot retention bonus program targeted at high-risk employees shows retention rates below 60%.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A pilot program shows retention rates at or above 60% for high-risk employees.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Retention rate among high-risk employees is between 40–60%.

ground_truth_rationale: The 60% retention rate comes from all bonus recipients, not specifically high-risk ones; this scope mismatch requires connecting the source of the estimate (stated) to its intended application (flight risk targeting). Medium.
notes: ROI assumption scope mismatch in HR analytics. Medium.

task_prompt: A marketing team used uplift modeling to estimate the incremental impact of their email campaign on purchase probability. The uplift model was trained on a historical A/B test dataset where 50,000 users were randomly assigned to receive or not receive a promotional email. The model achieves a Qini coefficient of 0.34 and the team targets the top 20% of users by predicted uplift for the next campaign. They claim the top-20% targeting will deliver an incremental revenue lift of $2.3M based on the model's predicted uplift scores applied to the expected recipient base of 500,000 users. The historical training data comes from campaigns run 18 months ago. The product catalog has since been substantially restructured. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim. Additional operational context from the review packet notes that the team documented data provenance, deployment timeline, and stakeholder ownership before presenting the result. Those details do not resolve the core methodological question; they simply explain why the team is confident enough to circulate the recommendation and why a reviewer could easily accept the narrative on first pass. The scenario is intended to read like a real internal ML review where practical rollout details sit alongside the analytic claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The uplift model was trained on historical campaign data from 18 months ago when the product catalog was different; applying the model to a restructured catalog assumes treatment effects are stable over time and across product contexts, which is unvalidated.

planted_issues.description: Uplift estimates from 18-month-old campaign data may not generalize to the current product catalog; treatment effects in uplift modeling are context-specific and catalog restructuring is a known driver of concept drift in marketing models.

ideal_critique: Uplift models estimate treatment effects for specific product contexts; a substantially restructured catalog changes the treatment effect distribution, potentially invalidating the 18-month-old model.
The $2.3M lift projection assumes static uplift estimates are portable to a new catalog, which is an unsupported domain generalization claim.
ideal_defense: The underlying email-response behavioral patterns (who responds to promotional emails) may be stable even if catalog changes; user-level uplift could remain predictive.
The Qini of 0.34 indicates the model captures genuine behavioral heterogeneity that is likely to persist.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A fresh A/B test on the new catalog shows the top-20%-uplift-targeted group performs no better than randomly selected recipients.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A fresh A/B test confirms the top-20% targeting still delivers statistically significant incremental lift.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Some lift is observed but well below the $2.3M projection.

ground_truth_rationale: Temporal generalization is medium because it requires recognizing that 18 months + catalog restructuring invalidates the uplift model's scope. Not directly stated as a flaw — requires connecting time gap and catalog change to model validity.
notes: Uplift model scope mismatch due to catalog drift. Medium.

task_prompt:
INTERNAL RESEARCH NOTE — FOR DISTRIBUTION TO PRODUCT AND STRATEGY TEAMS
Re: Causal Impact of On-Platform Learning Recommendations on User Skill Acquisition
Analytics Lead: Wei Zhang, Principal Data Scientist

This note summarizes our analysis of whether the platform's personalized learning recommendation engine causally accelerates skill acquisition among professional development users.

Study design: We used a difference-in-differences (DiD) approach, comparing skill assessment score improvements for users who received personalized recommendations (the treated group) versus users who used the browse/search feature exclusively (the comparison group). The analysis covers users active between March and September of the current year. Skill assessments are standardized 30-question tests taken voluntarily within the platform. We define skill gain as the improvement in post-minus-pre assessment score, normalized by pre-score.

To satisfy the parallel trends assumption required for DiD, we examined trends in skill assessment score improvement in the 90 days prior to each user's first recommendation exposure. Among treated users, the 90-day pre-period trend in skill gain was +0.8 points/month. Among comparison users (browse/search only), the pre-period trend was +0.9 points/month. These trends are not statistically different (p=0.31), confirming that treated and comparison users were on parallel trajectories before recommendation exposure.

DiD estimate: After the start of recommendation exposure, treated users showed an additional +4.2 points of skill gain relative to the comparison group over the 6-month evaluation period. This estimate is statistically significant (p < 0.001) and robust to controls for user tenure, industry category, and geographic region.

We conclude that personalized recommendations causally improve skill acquisition by approximately +4.2 normalized points over 6 months. This finding supports the strategic case for continued investment in the recommendation engine and forms the basis for our Q4 ROI projection submitted to the Executive Team.

Limitations acknowledged: We note the comparison group (browse/search users) may differ from the treated group in motivation level. However, the parallel trends validation above demonstrates that prior to recommendation exposure, both groups showed nearly identical learning trajectories, mitigating self-selection concerns. The appendix to the memo includes routine implementation details — staffing plans, rollout checkpoints, dashboard ownership, and escalation pathways — because the organization is preparing for deployment rather than for an academic publication. Those details are deliberately realistic but methodologically secondary. They create the texture of a credible internal review and force the reader to distinguish operational confidence from actual evidentiary support for the claim. ground_truth.final_verdict: The parallel trends test is insufficient for validating the DiD assumption when the treatment is endogenous: users who begin receiving recommendations may do so because they changed their engagement behavior (not randomly), and the 90-day pre-period test only checks for parallel trends on a specific outcome variable, not for time-varying confounders that activate simultaneously with recommendation exposure.

planted_issues.description: The parallel trends test compares pre-period outcome trends, but DiD causal identification fails when treatment onset is itself triggered by changes in unmeasured covariates that also affect outcomes; a user who begins receiving recommendations may have simultaneously changed engagement behavior in ways not captured by the pre-period skill trend.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowledge that parallel pre-trends is necessary but not sufficient for DiD causal identification when treatment timing is endogenous; the test rules out pre-existing level differences but not time-varying confounders that co-occur with treatment onset.

planted_issues.description: Skill assessments are voluntary; users who take post-period assessments are a selected subset, and recommendation exposure may differentially motivate users to take assessments, inflating the observed skill gain independently of true skill improvement.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires recognizing that assessment-taking is endogenous to recommendation engagement: users motivated by recommendations may be more likely to take assessments to measure progress, creating a collider bias in the measurement of skill gain.

planted_issues.description: Recommendation exposure begins when users engage enough to trigger personalization, making treatment onset itself a post-behavior event rather than an exogenous intervention boundary.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires causal inference knowledge about endogenous treatment timing.

ideal_critique: The parallel trends test is a necessary condition for DiD but not sufficient when treatment timing is endogenous; users who start receiving recommendations may do so because of behavioral changes that simultaneously affect learning rates.

Voluntary skill assessment introduces a selection mechanism: recommendation-exposed users may be more motivated to take assessments, inflating the observed skill gain through differential assessment participation rather than true skill improvement.
ideal_defense: The parallel trends test is the standard validation for DiD; the team has followed the appropriate practice for this method.
The control for motivation via pre-period trends is a reasonable attempt; the defense should propose an instrumental variable or natural experiment as the path to stronger causal claims.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Assessment participation rates diverge between treated and comparison groups in the post-period (i.e., treated users take more assessments), or a permutation test on placebo treatments in the pre-period shows spurious effects, suggesting the parallel trends assumption is fragile.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Assessment participation rates are balanced and a placebo DiD in the pre-period shows near-zero estimates, strengthening the case for causal identification.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Participation rates differ but sensitivity analysis on a balanced participation subset preserves the +4.2 estimate.

ground_truth_rationale: The parallel trends test is presented as validating causal identification, but its limitation (insufficient for endogenous treatment timing) requires advanced econometrics knowledge. The voluntary assessment issue adds a second flaw. The document's justification is subtly wrong: parallel trends is correct in principle but insufficient in this specific setup.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 6. Flaw type D (subtly wrong justification: parallel trends test) + C (critical omission: voluntary assessment selection). The document is about scope/intent (claiming causal impact from a non-random study) but the flaw is NOT the expected 'correlation vs causation' simple critique. The team explicitly addresses causation using DiD; the flaw is in the validity of the DiD parallel trends justification. Applying the standard scope/intent checklist ('did they confuse correlation with causation?') will produce the wrong answer — the team DID attempt to establish causation. Reviewers must then evaluate whether the DiD design actually achieves it. IDR: requires econometrics knowledge to evaluate the parallel trends test limitation. IDP: geographic controls insufficiency is a plausible but wrong red herring. DC: mixed verdict (two sides can defend) makes DRQ and ETD hard for single-pass. ETD: requires specifying a placebo DiD or participation rate test.
notes: Hard case. DiD parallel trends insufficient for endogenous treatment. Mixed position. Red herring: pre-period 90 days is reasonable; parallel trends test p=0.31 looks convincing. Must not claim: DiD requires randomization (wrong), geographic controls wrong.

planted_issues.description: Moving from email to checkout changes timing, urgency, and concurrent messaging, so the model’s ranking may not preserve the same treatment-effect ordering.

ideal_critique: The historical randomized experiment is valid evidence for one narrow claim: ranking users by responsiveness under the old email menu.

ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Demographic disaggregation shows minor differences but appeals mechanism resolves most cases quickly.

ground_truth_rationale: The absolute false positive volume calculation requires multiplying the stated precision by the stated volume; demographic disaggregation omission requires domain knowledge about NLP fairness. Medium: requires a computation and domain awareness.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The 23% higher spend among CLI recipients conflates the program effect with the selection effect: customers eligible for CLI (based on repayment history and utilization patterns) are systematically different from ineligible customers in ways that predict higher spending independently of the CLI.

planted_issues.description: CLI eligibility is based on repayment history and high utilization — both of which correlate with higher credit usage; comparing eligible/recipient customers to ineligible customers conflates the treatment effect with pre-existing spending propensity.

ideal_critique: Customers who qualify for CLI increases are higher spenders and higher utilizers by design of the eligibility criteria; comparing their post-CLI spend to ineligible customers does not isolate the CLI treatment effect.
The initial historical A/B test validation is the correct evaluation; the 12-month aggregate comparison is a confounded post-hoc analysis.
ideal_defense: The original A/B test validation is still valid; the post-hoc 23% difference is a monitoring metric, not the primary evidence for the program's effectiveness.
The team might note that the A/B test result should be the reference for expansion decisions, not the aggregate comparison.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Controlling for pre-CLI spending levels and utilization eliminates or substantially reduces the 23% spend difference.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A difference-in-differences analysis using the pre-CLI period as baseline confirms incremental spend attributable to the CLI.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Incremental spend remains but is substantially smaller (e.g., 8–10%) after controlling for selection.

ground_truth_rationale: CLI eligibility criteria (high utilization, good repayment) correlate with spending; connecting eligibility design to selection confound requires recognizing the self-fulfilling correlation. Medium.
notes: CLI selection confound in post-deployment evaluation. Medium.

task_prompt: DEPLOYMENT REVIEW — ED TRIAGE ASSIST MODEL NETWORK EXPANSION. The emergency medicine analytics program requests approval to expand its triage assist model from one flagship emergency department to all adult EDs in the network. The memo highlights an 81% exact-match rate against physician-assigned ESI-style acuity categories on retrospective data and emphasizes the operational benefit of faster waiting-room queue formation during peak hours. The system outputs one of five urgency levels and would be used to prioritize nurse assessment order when the waiting room is crowded. The evaluation packet reports overall accuracy and macro-F1 across the five levels, plus confusion matrices collapsed into adjacent-level versus non-adjacent-level errors. It notes that agreement with physicians is stable across hospitals and shift times. The memo argues this is the appropriate metric because triage is fundamentally a classification task and the model's purpose is to replicate clinician urgency ranking at scale. The text also states that only 5% of visits are Level 1 critical cases and 35% are Levels 4–5, with the remaining visits in the middle bands. The safety office is mentioned only briefly: if clinicians disagree with the model, clinicians prevail. No separate analysis is shown for Level-1 recall, door-to-provider time for critical patients, or what happens when the queue is ordered incorrectly under crowding. The rollout recommendation says these details can be monitored post-launch because the model is only assisting an existing nurse-led triage workflow rather than replacing it outright. However, in the same memo, operations leadership estimates the tool could save enough nurse time to justify using it as the first pass during overnight staffing shortages. Operational details were included in the review packet because the program is already resourced for launch: engineering capacity is allocated for the next release train, operations has staffed the monitoring rota, and finance has provisionally reserved budget contingent on a methods sign-off. The memo circulated among the data science review board, product leadership, and domain stakeholders. The writing tone is intentionally confident because the project has already passed infrastructure review and the remaining question is whether the evidence supports the claimed methodological conclusion. The document also notes that the team reviewed obvious concerns from prior launches: data source continuity, test environment stability, and reporting consistency. Those checks all passed and are presented as reassurance to stakeholders, which makes the remaining methodological flaw easy to miss on a quick read. Stakeholder comments in the appendix describe the result as strong enough for a phased rollout, but do not add new evidence. This operational confidence is part of the scenario noise: the methodology sounds polished and complete, and the critical issue is in an assumption or omission that is not obvious from surface-level review. ground_truth.final_verdict: Aggregate agreement metrics are not enough to justify deployment in a safety-critical triage workflow because the clinically meaningful question is Level-1 miss rate under crowding and queue-order consequences, but the assistive scope does make a staged pilot defensible if those safety slices are audited prospectively.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Prospectively shadow the tool and measure Level-1 recall, critical-patient wait times, and queue-order harm during high-crowding intervals before autonomous first-pass use.

planted_issues.description: Overall accuracy and macro-F1 do not characterize the catastrophic cost of under-triaging true Level-1 patients.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires emergency medicine knowledge about triage harm asymmetry.

planted_issues.description: Collapsing errors into adjacent vs non-adjacent categories ignores direction: a one-level downgrade for a critical patient can still be clinically dangerous while a one-level upgrade may be benign.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires triage-domain understanding that error direction matters, not just distance.

planted_issues.description: The memo frames the model as assistive, but the stated overnight staffing plan would effectively make it the first-pass queueing mechanism when clinician oversight is thinnest.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires operational knowledge about how workflow role changes alter deployment risk.

ideal_critique: The core problem is not that retrospective validation exists, but that the wrong deployment-facing metrics are reported for a queueing tool with extreme asymmetric harm.
Adjacent-error collapsing is a subtly wrong justification because direction of error matters in triage.
The rollout memo quietly shifts from assistive use to first-pass overnight use, which changes the evidentiary burden.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue that as a shadow-mode or advisory tool, the model may still be worth piloting if critical-slice auditing is added.
The defense should reject autonomous or first-pass overnight use until the missing safety metrics are prospectively measured.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Prospective shadow-mode evaluation shows Level-1 recall or critical-patient queue times are materially worse than clinician-only triage during high-crowding intervals.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Prospective shadow-mode evaluation shows near-perfect Level-1 recall and no worsening of critical-patient queue times, supporting staged assistive deployment.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Shadow-mode performance is acceptable for advisory use but not strong enough for overnight first-pass queueing.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a mixed-position deployment case: the existing evidence is insufficient for the stronger overnight first-pass use case, but an assistive pilot could be defensible. Hardness comes from the subtle role shift and the need to specify the exact prospective safety audit.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 8. Type D/C/A. The obvious concern is simply “retrospective data,” but the real issue is metric and workflow mismatch under asymmetric harm. Single-pass reviewers often fail DRQ and ETD by not distinguishing advisory deployment from first-pass use.
notes: Hard RWF case. Domain expertise: emergency triage. Mixed empirical-test case with 3 must-find issues and plausible red herrings.

task_prompt: AML GOVERNANCE PACKET — ALERT MODERNIZATION DECISION. The financial crime analytics team is seeking approval to replace the legacy rule system with a machine-learning alert ranker in the transaction-monitoring program. The memo opens with strong operational results: suspicious activity report yield rose from 31% to 42% during a monitored pilot, analysts closed fewer low-value cases, and average investigation time per filed SAR fell by 18%. The document frames this as evidence that the new system is better at detecting money laundering while also lowering analyst burden. The model does not auto-file SARs; it ranks transactions and cases for analyst review. Ground truth in the packet is defined by SAR filings following analyst investigation. The queue design section explains that analysts prioritize alerts with larger dollar value, cross-border movement, and known-risk typologies because these are more likely to generate recoverable loss or regulatory importance. Low-dollar domestic alerts are often closed quickly when queue pressure is high. The memo argues that SAR yield is the right deployment metric because investigators are the bottleneck and regulators care that filed SARs are well supported. What it does not show is any audit of unreviewed or quickly closed alerts, nor any estimate of what fraction of true laundering patterns remain unflagged. The recommendation asks to expand the ML ranker and also lower staffing in the low-priority queue because the higher SAR yield is taken as evidence that those cases are mostly noise. ground_truth.final_verdict: Higher SAR yield supports a claim about analyst-efficiency in the reviewed queue, but it does not establish superior laundering detection coverage; using SAR yield alone to justify queue reduction or broad effectiveness claims is not methodologically sound without an audit of missed risk in low-priority alerts.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a stratified random audit of low-priority and closed alerts to estimate missed laundering risk and compare total-detection coverage under the legacy and ML-ranked queue policies.

planted_issues.description: SAR yield is a precision-like measure on investigated cases and does not measure coverage of true laundering activity outside the reviewed queue.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires AML operations knowledge about queue-based monitoring and partial ground truth.

planted_issues.description: Changing which alerts receive analyst attention also changes which cases can become SAR-confirmed, so the metric itself is endogenous to staffing and prioritization policy.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding of operational label generation in compliance workflows.

planted_issues.description: The memo uses higher SAR yield to justify cutting low-priority queue staffing, but no audit estimates how much laundering risk remains in the deprioritized cases.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires AML regulatory knowledge about coverage obligations beyond yield.

ideal_critique: A calibrated critique should acknowledge that yield improvement is meaningful for analyst efficiency while rejecting the stronger claim that it proves better total detection coverage.
Because SAR labels are produced by analyst review, changing queue policy changes the observed label distribution itself.
The staffing-reduction recommendation is the part most clearly unsupported without a random audit of deprioritized alerts.
ideal_defense: The defense can reasonably say SAR yield is an important operational metric when analysts are the bottleneck and the system is a triage tool rather than an autonomous detector.
The defense should narrow the claim to queue efficiency and accept that coverage claims still need a stratified audit.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A stratified audit of deprioritized alerts finds non-trivial laundering prevalence or typologies missed by the ML-ranked queue, undermining the staffing-cut proposal.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A stratified audit shows very low missed laundering prevalence in deprioritized alerts while reviewed-queue SAR quality remains higher than legacy.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The ML system improves reviewed-queue efficiency but also misses some low-value typologies, supporting partial deployment without queue cuts.

ground_truth_rationale: The memo contains a genuine two-sided argument: SAR yield matters operationally, but it is insufficient for the broader staffing and coverage claims. Hardness comes from recognizing the endogenous label-generation mechanism and specifying the audit needed to settle the disagreement.
difficulty_justification: Principles 1, 4, 8. Type A/D/C. This is harder than a generic precision-vs-recall complaint because the metric is genuinely useful for one narrow purpose. Reviewers must produce a calibrated mixed verdict and a concrete audit design.
notes: Hard RWF case with mixed empirical-test resolution and AML domain knowledge.
